links.content: brunolemos authored issue #13991.
brunolemos reports that having multiple instances of React by mistake leads to the incorrect error: 'hooks can only be called inside the body of a function component'.
Hooks functionality is affected by multiple instances of React.
When React tries to pass data to a custom element it always does so using HTML attributes.
React was one of the first JavaScript frameworks jb3 looked at.
React allowed VictorJozwicki to grow as a developer.
React changed the way cem2ran views UI programming and Open Source.
React depends on `requestAnimationFrame` (even in test environments).
Since React 16, if an unknown key/value pair is passed, it will be treated as an attribute.

links.content: GabrielBB experienced the same error when using `npm link` with a package that uses its own React instance.
Regular NPM installs will continue to use the 15.6 release.
mpeyper reports that Lerna monorepos suffer from the same issue when a custom hook is defined in one package and used by another, due to symlinked dependencies using their own copy of React.
Lerna monorepos can use `npm-link-shared` as a workaround for multiple React instances.
vpicone reports having the same issue with `npm-link` even with exact same React versions, receiving the unhelpful 'hooks can only be called inside the body of a function component' message.
dotlouis reports the same issue when using `npm link` with a package to their main application.
zbuttram experienced this error when running React and Rails with the `react-rails` gem and rendering components into Rails views.
The `react-rails` gem can be used with Turbolinks.
taylorham reports that the issue is still relevant in the released version of Hooks with the same unhelpful error message.

RFC: Plan for custom element attributes/properties in React 19
Option 1: Only set properties is proposed for handling custom element attributes/properties.
Option 5: An API for consuming custom elements is proposed for handling custom element attributes/properties.
robdodson prefers option 3.
robdodson agrees that naming collisions are a weakness in Web Components standards.
robdodson will discuss naming collisions at the upcoming TPAC standards meeting.
robdodson disagrees with effulgentsia's concern about developers needing to know when to use an attribute vs. a property, stating developers would likely use the sigil for consistency.
jeremenichelli is between option 2 and option 3, finding option 3 attractive.
jeremenichelli agrees that React shouldn't worry about consumers knowing whether an element has implemented something as a property or an attribute.
jeremenichelli agrees there isn't a one-size-fits-all rule for attributes/properties and documentation is necessary.
jeremenichelli likes the idea of property binding.
cjorasch questions if consumers of a custom element need to understand the distinction between attribute and property binding for Option 2.
cjorasch finds it confusing to bind differently based on the value in Option 2.

The rewrite aimed to ship requested features like returning arrays from render.
The rewrite aimed to ship requested features like readable component stack traces for every error.
The rewrite aimed to enable experimenting with asynchronous rendering of components for better perceived performance.
dmitrif asks for suggestions on how to make `electrode-react-ssr-caching` work with React v16.
`electrode-react-ssr-caching` relies on `react-dom/lib/ReactCompositeComponent` and `react-dom/lib/DOMPropertyOperations`, which are no longer available in React 16.
aweary suggests integrating a caching solution directly into the server renderer.
verkholantsev asks if there is a way to manually enable asynchronous rendering in this beta.
TrySound believes there is or will be an option for asynchronous rendering.

React Fire is an effort to modernize React DOM.
The goal of React Fire is to make React better aligned with how the DOM works.
The goal of React Fire is to revisit some controversial past decisions that led to problems.
The goal of React Fire is to make React smaller and faster.
The set of changes for React Fire will be shipped in a future React major release.
The reflection of input values in the `value` attribute was originally added in React 15.2.0 via PR 6406.

mknepprath expresses support for React Fire.
nhunzaker highlights the re-write of the event system as the most interesting aspect, seeing opportunity for bundle size reduction and easier community contributions.
nhunzaker authored the pull request 'Allow custom attributes by default'.
nhunzaker noted that `npm test` locally was not running the server-side tests.
nhunzaker implemented the same approach for custom events in a separate pull request.
nhunzaker believes the work on custom events might be all of the outstanding work for stripping away nearly all of the attribute whitelist.
nhunzaker stripped the ARIA list, moving props to developer warnings.
nhunzaker proposed consolidating the ARIAHook into the UnknownProperty hook.
nhunzaker proposed allowing the attribute whitelist to be externally configurable to purge it from the build.
nhunzaker proposed setting up CI to run the test suite with both conditions of the new `allowCustomAttributes` flag, similar to the Fiber flag.
kentcdodds suggests releasing JSX 2.0 at the same time as React Fire changes to batch re-learning for users.

sizebot authored comment 1 on issue #14853.
A pre-release build of React DevTools is available for smoke testing related to Issue 20463.
The feature to display serialized args from console.(warn|error) in element tree view and inspected element view is currently implemented in React DevTools.
The visuals for displaying serialized args match those of Chrome DevTools.
The feature to clear warnings (all, per element, per warning via context menu) is currently implemented in React DevTools.
The feature for stepping through warnings is currently implemented in React DevTools.
DevTools are not injected on initial page render, causing early logged errors or warnings to be missed.
The asynchronous `act(...)` preserves the synchronous API.
The asynchronous `act(...)` is less restrictive than the synchronous model.
The asynchronous `act(...)` exposes a secret API on React DOM.
The refactoring of `act(...)` logic was optimized for ReactDOM's bundle size.
A warning gets triggered if the result from `act` is not `await`ed.
It is possible to nest `act()` calls.
A cheap form of unrolling safety is implemented for `act()` calls.
The `act` API is not specifically for `ReactDOM.render`.
The `act(...)` implementation now uses `ReactFiberScheduler::flushPassiveEffects`.
The `act(...)` implementation now recursively flushes effects.
The `act(...)` implementation fixes bug #14954.
The `act(...)` code cannot be moved outside ReactFiberScheduler.
The `act(...)` logic was refactored to pull most logic out of FiberScheduler.
The `act(...)` implementation passes `doesHavePendingPassiveEffects` as an argument to the internal callback.
The `act(...)` implementation uses an `onDone` callback for cleanup.
thysultan authored comment 7 on issue #14853.
thysultan proposed improving async behavior for ReactDOM.render by invoking a render callback when work is done and returning a thenable.
Sebmarkbage states that implicit syntax for multiple components will probably never be allowed.
Sebmarkbage suggests explicit wrappers like `<></>` or `[]` for returning multiple components.
The pull request 'Allow custom attributes by default' was closed in favor of pull request #10385.
The pull request 'Allow custom attributes by default' is a resubmission of pull request #6459.
The pull request 'Allow custom attributes by default' was prompted by the addition of the unsupported property warning (#6800).
The pull request 'Allow custom attributes by default' configures DOMPropertyOperations to allow all attributes to pass through.
The pull request 'Allow custom attributes by default' maintains the whitelist only for attributes that need special behavior.
nhunzaker reverted the property whitelists back to their original state in the pull request.

The pull request 'Allow custom attributes by default' eliminated duplicated 'reserved props' list between `ReactDOMUnknownPropertyDevtool` and `ReactDOMComponent` through a new `isReservedProp` method on `DOMProperty`.
The pull request 'Allow custom attributes by default' is related to issue #9594.
The pull request 'Allow custom attributes by default' required changes for Fiber.

The pull request 'Allow custom attributes by default' addresses several items from issue #9477, including adding new behavior of falling back to setAttribute for unknown properties, ensuring Fiber tests pass, and verifying server rendering test suite.
The full HTML and SVG property lists have been added to the UnknownProperty hook.
nhunzaker adjusted the logic inside of UnknownDOMPropertyHook to support warnings on bad casing while ignoring custom attributes when the `allowCustomAttributes` flag is true.
nhunzaker added a new flag `allowCustomAttributes` to the pull request.
nhunzaker added a new `isWriteableAttribute` method on `DOMProperty` that replaces the existing `isCustomAttribute` predicate.
The pull request 'Allow custom attributes by default' is labeled as 'Component: DOM'.
Internal callsites are the biggest blocker for this feature, causing 'unknown prop' warnings.
sophiebits agreed with gaearon's assessment of internal callsites being the biggest blocker.
Sophiebits doesn't think the fragment API is particularly controversial.

Syranide had a brief chat with chenglou about the issue.
Syranide sees hidden dangers in allowing composite components to return multiple components.
Syranide suggests that perhaps it's simply a case of developer responsibility and that there may be good use-cases for both, and they should look past inevitable misuse.
Syranide suggests returning an array as a workaround for the fragment API.
Syranide mentioned sebmarkbage regarding comments on the issue.
Syranide mentions a custom `<Frag>` component as a workaround for the fragment API.
Syranide suggests `return <NoopComp>...</NoopComp>.props.children` as a workaround for the fragment API.
AdamKyle requests a fragment API to allow returning multiple components from render.
AdamKyle reports an error: 'Adjacent XJS elements must be wrapped in an enclosing tag' when removing wrapper divs.
AdamKyle reports that the current behavior adds extra, useless, and pointless HTML to the page.
The fragment API is a hard problem with React's current architecture.
AdamKyle asks if the extra HTML affects libraries like jQuery or CSS selectors.
DAddYE confirms that the extra HTML affects CSS selectors.
Chenglou explains the error occurs because not putting a wrapper desugars to `React.DOM.div(...)React.DOM.div(...)`, which is syntactically incorrect.
Chenglou mentioned syranide regarding comments on the issue.
Gabssnake reports spending time understanding why their component didn't work due to the wrapping requirement.
Gabssnake suggests there should be a notice in the React documentation about the wrapping requirement.
GetContented expresses pain with the current behavior, especially for designers.
GetContented wishes a component could output a node (node-list or fragment) instead of an element.
The feature to desugar to `[div, div]` is hard, somewhat controversial, and won't be implemented in the near future.
The fragment API will probably not be supported short term.
This issue is meant to address #7249.
Because attributes must be serialized to strings, this approach creates problems when the data being passed is an object or array.
It might be the case that on the server, the sigil would fallback to setting an attribute.
effulgentsia thinks Option 1 would be the most idiomatic if backwards compatibility was not a concern.
effulgentsia proposes solving the server-side rendering problem by providing an API for application code to inform React on how to map custom element properties to attributes.
effulgentsia finds Option 3 appealing if backwards compatibility is a concern.
effulgentsia suggests using "." as the sigil for Option 3.
effulgentsia finds it unfortunate to make every instance of a custom element responsible for knowing when to use an attribute vs. a property.
effulgentsia believes that a sigil fallback to setting an attribute on the server would not work if the property does not exist as an attribute (e.g., video's `currentTime`).
effulgentsia proposes a `ReactDOM.defineCustomElementProp()` API for mapping custom element properties to attributes.
effulgentsia prefers the centralization possibility of `ReactDOM.defineCustomElementProp()` over requiring every user to be explicit with a sigil.
